title: Senior-Living Ministry
metaTitle: A Scripture-First Formation App & Admin Portal · Artifact Digital
description: Two live products for a senior-living ministry — a Scripture-first daily formation app for residents aged 70+, and an admin portal that lets non-technical staff run it without a developer.

card.blurb: A Scripture-first daily formation app for senior-living residents, plus an admin portal that lets ministry staff run it without a developer.
card.imageAlt: A senior-living resident reading a Scripture passage in the daily formation app on her phone.

heroEyebrow: Case study · Mobile app & admin platform
lede: Two live products for a senior-living ministry: a Scripture-first daily formation app for residents, staff and the surrounding community — and a web admin portal that lets non-technical ministry staff run the content, events, messaging and people behind it.
pillarsHeading: A digital front door, for an audience the internet forgot.

pillars.title: No front door
pillars.body: Scripture, devotionals, study, events, media and photos lived in scattered places — or nowhere — for a community that is largely 70 and over.

pillars.title: Accessibility was the constraint
pillars.body: Small type, low contrast, tiny targets and feature buffets fail this audience specifically. It shaped every decision we made.

pillars.title: Staff couldn't change anything
pillars.body: Content, media, livestreams, events and resources were all code-level. Nothing moved without a developer.

storyParagraphs: The first brief pointed at YouVersion. It's the category leader, so it's the natural reference — and it was exactly wrong. YouVersion is dense, young, gamified and feature-forward. A pastoral senior-living ministry should feel like the opposite: warm, unhurried, quiet. Everything got better the moment we threw the comparison out and started from the ministry's own identity instead. Competitor-shaped is not the same as user-shaped.

The home screen had eight tiles and six competing cards. We cut it to one primary action — "Continue Reading," set over Scripture imagery. That single act of subtraction did more for the product than any feature we could have added. For an audience of 70+, the question isn't "what can they do?" It's "do they know what to do first?"

The architectural decision that carried everything was a provider seam: content flows through swappable providers, and the whole thing runs, demos and deploys on a mock data layer designed as a drop-in for a real database. Full SQL migrations and row-level security policies are written and waiting. That let us ship something real and shareable before any backend existed — which is also the honest limitation. It's a fully-formed prototype without a persistent spine. Connecting the database is the difference between demo and product, and we say so plainly.

One more decision worth naming: we bundled the entire KJV — all 66 books, 4.3 MB — instead of calling a Bible API. That removed the network, rate limits, licensing exposure and latency in a single move. It works in airplane mode. The primitive choice was the right one.

highlights.title: Two live products, ~40 screens
highlights.body: A full mobile app and an admin portal, both deployable and shareable by URL, with clean production builds and zero TypeScript errors.

highlights.title: The Bible works fully offline
highlights.body: The entire KJV bundled — no network, no rate limits, no licensing exposure. It works on a plane.

highlights.title: Real audio Bible via on-device TTS
highlights.body: Verse-by-verse follow-along highlighting with voice and speed selection, working on iOS, Android and web.

highlights.title: An admin portal staff can actually run
highlights.body: 11 sections, five roles with server-enforced access, audit logging, and a Media + Resources CMS with scheduling, bulk actions and import/export.

ctaHeading: Building for an audience everyone else designs past?

feature.alt: The ministry admin portal dashboard, showing sample engagement data across campuses
feature.caption: The admin portal staff actually run. Figures shown are sample data — the product has no users yet.

storyImageAlt: Managing an event in the admin portal — registrants, details and messaging
storyCaption: Events, content and messaging — all editable by ministry staff, no developer required.
